channel_name: The Compound and Friends · Josh Brown
gist: BMO-alum strategist Brian Belski (now founder/CIO of Humilis) stays structurally bullish on a multi-year secular bull but warns the 'second derivative' of earnings growth is turning less positive, driving a choppier H2 and a durable rotation out of mega-cap AI winners into SMID, regional banks, value and beaten-down software. He entertains a genuine bear case (capex reversal simultaneously nuking earnings and multiples, a possible eventual recession) but leans on AI demand/scarcity to stay long.

insights.claim: Belski stays bullish with the S&P 500 at all-time highs by year-end but frames the key H2 risk as the 'second derivative less positive' — decelerating (not negative) earnings growth that spooks a data-obsessed market into selling.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski (CIO, Humilis)
insights.so_what: Positions for a grind-higher-but-choppy tape; fade sharp selloffs on decelerating-growth headlines rather than de-risking outright.
insights.quote: beware of the second derivative less positive

insights.claim: Belski's framework holds that earnings-driven markets deliver only ~10-12% annual returns, far below the 20-25% momentum/multiple-driven gains of the past two years, so investors should reset return expectations downward even in a bull market.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: A durable de-rating of return expectations argues for stock-picking and rotation over beta-chasing.

insights.claim: Belski argues a slide in S&P earnings growth from ~25% to ~20% is enough to trigger a selloff even though growth stays strong, because falling estimates make investors extrapolate toward worst-case scenarios.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Watch the rate-of-change of forward EPS revisions, not the level, as the trigger for volatility.

insights.claim: Belski calls the market over-bullish and expects a choppier second half than consensus, noting the paradox that even the bulls are all calling for a correction.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Crowded bullish-but-hedged positioning can whipsaw; expect volatility without a durable top.
insights.quote: there's too many bulls out there

insights.claim: Belski expects year four of the current bull cycle to be the most volatile — pushed out one year from the usual year-three peak volatility by 2025's manufactured 'Liberation Day' turmoil and the Middle East war.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Sets expectations for elevated 2026 volatility as a normal cyclical feature, not a regime break.

insights.claim: Belski maintains his 25-year secular bull thesis with 7-10 years left to run, keeping a structurally long bias despite near-term caution.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Anchors dips as buyable within a long-horizon uptrend.
insights.quote: I'm still on board with my 25 year secular bull

themes.theme: Mega-cap AI: hyperscaler handoff and capex risk

insights.claim: Belski sees a leadership handoff from owning hyperscalers/data-center buyers to owning the companies selling products and services into the AI buildout, with the MAG7 themselves losing investor favor.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Tilt toward AI-supply-chain and picks-and-shovels names over the mega-cap platform owners.

insights.claim: Most MAG7 names are mid-teens to mid-20% off their highs and have been a net detractor from the S&P 500's year-to-date return until a late bounce, undercutting the 'index is only up because of mega-cap' narrative.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Breadth, not mega-cap, is carrying the tape — supportive of the broadening trade.

insights.claim: Belski is sticking with Microsoft, arguing a ~23-24% YTD drawdown makes no sense against 34% ROE, ~$78B cash on the balance sheet and consistent ~25% quarterly earnings growth; the stock got trapped in a 'biggest software stock' narrative.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Frames MSFT weakness as a sentiment/liquidity dislocation, not fundamental deterioration — a buy.

insights.claim: Belski holds Oracle as 'unjustly punished,' arguing it still trades on AI/OpenAI fears (the doubted 5-year ~$300B contract) despite being an early AI adopter that re-rated from a value tech name.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Oracle is a high-beta way to bet AI-infrastructure fears are overdone; note it kept falling even as the group bounced.

insights.claim: Belski is 'not a believer' in Meta's pivot to renting out excess compute (a NeoCloud), seeing it as flailing that undercuts the AI-shortage narrative; the 10% pop faded fast.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Fade Meta rallies driven by compute-rental headlines; the market re-rated it lower within a day.

insights.claim: The hosts and Belski agree Meta is the best AI monetizer via advertising and an addictive Reels/Instagram product, but a poor capital allocator — its metaverse push spent ~$80B and lost ~$80B.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown) and guest
insights.so_what: Great cash engine, bad reinvestment record — the recurring reason the stock is cheap and hated between blowout prints.
insights.quote: They spent $80 billion and lost $80 billion

insights.claim: Since 'Liberation Day' Meta is down ~18% while Google is up ~100%, and Belski calls Google the 'pick to click' over Meta despite Meta trading at only ~16-17x earnings.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Prefer Alphabet to Meta among the ad-driven mega-caps; the two have decoupled from their historically tight correlation.

insights.claim: Belski argues hyperscaler AI capex 'has to slow down' eventually, but reads Google issuing stock after a decade of buybacks as a signal that spend is set to accelerate, not decelerate — companies willing to take free cash flow to zero must tap markets.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Equity/debt issuance by hyperscalers is a tell that capex intensity is still rising near-term.

themes.theme: Broadening and the SMID / small-cap rotation

insights.claim: Belski cites next-12-month earnings growth of ~28% for small caps (S&P 600/SML) versus ~23-24% for large caps as a fundamental case for the SMID rotation.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Superior forward EPS growth, not just rate hopes, underpins small-cap leadership.

insights.claim: All publicly traded US small- and mid-cap companies combined add up to roughly the weight of Apple alone, illustrating extreme index concentration.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Any real mean-reversion in concentration is a large tailwind for the entire SMID universe.
insights.quote: they add up to the weight of Apple

insights.claim: The top 25 US stocks have grown from ~35% of the index in 1997 to ~54% today — 25 names are now roughly half the US stock market.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Concentration risk cuts both ways: outsized index sensitivity to a handful of names.

insights.claim: Belski argues the rising number of year-over-year gainers (~350 S&P names up, roughly half the index posting double-digit gains) is healthy broadening and historically inconsistent with a market top — with an asterisk that 2021 was a stimulus-driven exception.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Broadening breadth is a bullish tell; treat 2021's everything-rally as a non-comparable outlier.
insights.quote: You don't have a market top as it's broadening

insights.claim: The hosts flag a paradox: the Russell 2000 is beating the equal-weight S&P even as the largest S&P names lead within the index and the smallest decile lags — implying leadership is a sector story, not a size story.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Screen by sector (AI-adjacent winners in each) rather than by market-cap bucket.

insights.claim: The 2026 small-cap surge is unusually large by historical standards — small-cap-over-large-cap rallies have lasted only ~3-6 months over the past decade — yet Belski thinks this one can persist through year-end, especially if the 10-year Treasury yield falls.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: A lower 10-year is the key catalyst to extend an already-stretched small-cap run; watch rates.

insights.claim: Belski prefers the S&P SmallCap 600 (SML) and mid-cap over the Russell 2000, because the Russell is loaded with non-earning, high-beta names, whereas the SML/mid screen better on price-to-free-cash-flow and earnings consistency.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Choose quality-tilted SMID benchmarks over the Russell for cleaner exposure to the rotation.

insights.claim: Belski's favored SMID themes include Acuity (data-center cooling ceiling tiles), FNB (a Western Pennsylvania regional bank), and Amphenol (a ~century-old signals/connector company) as under-covered AI-adjacent and migration plays.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Names to research as lesser-known ways to play data-center buildout and regional growth.

insights.claim: Belski runs a barbell in banks: the very biggest and the very smallest win while mid-size regionals are stuck in the middle, lacking both scale and relationship power.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Avoid the squeezed middle; own money-center scale players and niche community banks.

insights.claim: Belski argues small banks are winning relationship-driven business from the giants in fast-growing, tax-advantaged regions (Montana, Wyoming, Idaho, the Southeast), citing owning Glacier Bancorp and a Southeast bank that was acquired by Pinnacle.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Population/business migration is a screen for winning community banks and buyout candidates.

insights.claim: Belski expects a wave of 'super-regional' bank consolidation with the White House allowing deals, naming PNC and Citizens as aggressive acquirers and predicting more Midwest bank combinations.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: M&A optionality favors mid-size regionals as targets; a friendlier antitrust stance is the catalyst.

insights.claim: Belski calls the exchanges cheap and oversold, with Josh Brown buying CME and flagging CBOE, ICE, Nasdaq, S&P Global and Moody's as crushed on an overdone reaction to Kalshi/perpetual-listing competitive fears.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown) and guest
insights.so_what: Exchange stocks offer a contrarian entry if the perpetual-listing/prediction-market threat is overstated.

insights.claim: Within financials Belski flags Unum insurance and Schwab as interesting, and says his contrarian instinct wants Blackstone but the headlines will stay bad; Blue Owl bounced on less-bad-than-feared redemptions (~19% vs ~25% prior).
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Private-market financials are trading on perceived AI/software distress; wait for sentiment to turn.

insights.claim: Guggenheim's John DiFucci calls the deep software selloff one of the best opportunities for patient investors in his career — arguing traditional software will at least persist even as it trades as if it will decline into perpetuity — and upgrades names including ServiceNow.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown, relaying DiFucci)
insights.so_what: A high-profile upgrade thesis; IGV already up ~11% off lows, outpacing the MAG7 bounce.
insights.quote: one of the best opportunities for patient investors in our careers

insights.claim: The hosts reframe the software drawdown as multiple normalization rather than an AI 'SaaS apocalypse': ServiceNow trades ~22.9x forward vs a 5-year average of ~55x, and Salesforce ~11x forward vs a ~30x average.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Valuations have reset toward normal; the debate is whether growth stabilizes, not whether the businesses die.

insights.claim: Software rallies every time the AI trade hiccups, making it the best hedge for AI-trade worries — but it is rallying less and falling more on each successive reversal, signaling waning conviction.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Use software as an AI-trade hedge, but its diminishing bounces warn the pair-trade is losing potency.

insights.claim: Belski is skeptical enterprises still need Salesforce and Adobe long-term and doubts Salesforce growth will accelerate, making him wary of the whole big-cap SaaS complex even at reset multiples.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Cheap alone isn't enough for legacy SaaS; Belski would rather own Oracle/Microsoft than distressed SaaS.

insights.claim: Belski predicts index providers (Russell, S&P, MSCI) will break the technology sector apart again within 2-3 years — potentially splitting out semiconductors, hardware and software (software is only ~7% of the index).
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: A GICS reclassification would reshape sector ETFs and passive flows within tech.

insights.claim: Belski volunteers that the US will have a recession 'at some point,' noting there hasn't been a normal, run-of-the-mill recession since 2001 and questioning whether the modern economy can even produce an old-fashioned inventory-driven one.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: A rare cautious note from a structural bull; watch for the first classic cyclical downturn in 20+ years.
insights.quote: I think we're going to have a recession at some point

insights.claim: The core bear case articulated is that when AI capex trends reverse, they simultaneously 'nuke' earnings for every company feasting on the spend AND compress multiples — a double hit compounded by a reverse wealth effect on consumer spending.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: The real tail risk isn't a demand air-pocket alone but earnings and multiples derating together plus a consumer pullback.

insights.claim: The hosts raise the 1999 analogy: AI technology may be only the 2nd inning, but the stock market could already be in the 8th, having discounted the entire opportunity ahead of the real-economy adoption.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Being right on the technology's longevity doesn't protect you if the market has already priced it — the key valuation risk.

insights.claim: Josh Brown argues AI capex won't stop 'on a dime' because the cost of a token is falling ~90% while token usage grows ~5x faster, and spend is now tied to booked AI revenue rather than speculative buildout.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Revenue-linked, demand-outrunning-supply economics argue against an abrupt capex collapse — the bull rebuttal.
insights.quote: The cost of a token is declining rapidly like 90%

insights.claim: Belski leans on scarcity — in compute and memory — as the reason to stay long, arguing that if AI is truly only in the second inning the market runs 'a lot higher' than a hypothetical S&P 10,000.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Trade the scarcity in AI compute/memory; Belski's upside case is materially above current levels.
insights.quote: you never doubt scarcity always trade on scarcity always buy scarcity

insights.claim: Belski frames Netflix as a 'cash, content and consolidation' story that gets killed roughly every four years and always comes back better; he added it to his value portfolio around a $93 average cost.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: Treat periodic Netflix drawdowns as recurring value entries within a media-consolidation thesis.

insights.claim: Josh Brown owns Netflix but argues this drawdown is harder than 2022 because Netflix has already won streaming (the easy levers of ad-tier and password-sharing are spent) and now faces YouTube as the 'final dragon' it can't easily beat — shifting the investor base from growth to value.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Subscriber-growth phase is over; re-rate Netflix as a maturing value name, not a growth compounder.
insights.quote: like YouTube is the final dragon and I don't know how they beat YouTube

insights.claim: Belski predicts Netflix makes a run at NBCUniversal via the Comcast spinout — including the theme parks and a top-three film library — and thinks the street would cheer the deal even though subscriber growth is done.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: An NBCU/parks bid is a potential positive catalyst, unlike the poorly received Warner pursuit.

insights.claim: Belski calls communication services the likely surprise sector of H2 2026, favoring Netflix, AT&T and Spotify despite the sector being a year-to-date laggard (AT&T, Verizon weak).
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: A beaten-down sector Belski expects to outperform; screen comm-services laggards for reversals.

insights.claim: The hosts note Netflix is now too big to be acquired, so it must be the acquirer — structurally forcing it into large M&A to sustain growth.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Expect Netflix to keep pursuing transformative deals; each bid is now a share-price event.

themes.theme: Structural decliners: cable, telecom, airlines, and hidden value

insights.claim: Belski calls Charter Communications 'the worst stock in the world' and won't own it at any price given too much debt and secular decline, comparing it to Boise Cascade — no PE multiple can rescue it without an operational turnaround he doesn't see coming.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: A hard avoid; bounces on Comcast news gave everything back within days.
insights.quote: This is the worst stock in the world

insights.claim: Josh Brown argues Starlink offering competitively priced broadband from space within ~2 years would be the 'last nail in the coffin' for last-mile cable/telecom providers, as hyperscalers buy satellite capacity.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Satellite broadband is an existential threat to last-mile incumbents; a reason to stay short/avoid the group.

insights.claim: The hosts argue blocking airline mergers backfires — stopping JetBlue/Spirit just let Spirit fail and handed its routes to Delta — reinforcing an oligopoly of Delta and United, with Belski owning American in his small/mid-cap sleeve.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown) and guest
insights.so_what: Consolidation accrues to the surviving majors regardless of antitrust; own the winners of forced consolidation.

insights.claim: Belski contends Waymo should be spun out with its own board, CEO and share capitalization, and would likely be bundled with Google Maps for consumers — while YouTube, a cash cow, will never be spun out of Alphabet.
insights.speaker: guest, Brian Belski
insights.so_what: A Waymo carve-out (not YouTube) is the more plausible Alphabet value-unlock catalyst to watch.
